replies.title: avoiding egocentrism
replies.content:
Getting some perspective on your own level of attainment can happen from reading difficult books as JMG says. It can also come from direct contact with other people who are fully engaged with the same pursuit, discovering that they have forgotten more than you ever learned, and that you would fit right in with the half dozen students in their advanced beginner course. Or, at long last, after toiling in isolation for a long time, you finally get to meet some peers to talk shop with, and they are making jokes of the kind that come from relaxed familiarity about something you Take Very Seriously.

There is no substitute for the solitary grind, but I think balancing that by contact with colleagues is healthy. If you can find a community that maintains some standards without cutthroat competition, this can be very pleasant and it prevents you from having to go up a hat size.

All of that is true in any occupation that requires both some talent and a lot of application. Some fields of magic have specific dangers. One is that since it is a fringe interest in our culture, anybody with a bit of charisma and half a clue can set themselves up as a teacher and gather acolytes. If somehow none of the acolytes ever go on to set up their own independent groups while remaining on good terms with their former teacher, that's a teacher to stay away from.

Paths that encourage identification with a deity as a part of their practice attract people who suffer from low self esteem and want to repair themselves. A little of this can do some good if it is kept within bounds. Too much can lead to making a fool of yourself, or being a PITA to your friends (hopefully a temporary phase) or in serious cases, ego inflation that is compensatory and does not ever come to terms with the underlying psychological problem. Sooner or later, you must acquire self knowledge or you will not progress.

replies.title: Re: avoiding egocentrism
replies.content: How would you go about finding colleagues in this pursuit? It often feels like a lonely path but I wouldn't even know where to look for like-minded people.

replies.title: Re: avoiding egocentrism
replies.content:
I can tell you how I did it but you will have to do things differently, because times have changed.

I got started in the pre-Internet days when things were less out in the open. The way I made my first contacts was by seeing a couple of zines on sale in a feminist bookstore that related to my interests, buying them, reading them, and writing private letters to the editors. One of the magazines was published in a far off state, and they sent me a rudimentary correspondence course for awhile. The other zine was published near to where I live. Its editors replied to me and two of them arranged to meet me in a public place. I poured out my heart, and they put me in touch with a local group that was doing something related to my interests. This group was ethical and highly networked in the local Pagan community.

Once I had that first introduction, a world opened up to me. That only happened because I was fortunate enough to live in a place where the various Pagans and occultists got along with each other and weren't playing dominance games, and there were diverse groups.

I had opportunities to join some groups, but being the sort of person I am, I had some authority issues, so I studied a little while longer on my own and then gathered a few like-minded women to study together in an egalitarian fashion. I should say that by the time I attempted that, I had read dozens of books that were recommended to me; I just didn't know how to do anything. Meeting in a small group for three years gave me enough experience and basic knowledge to move on. I went through the swelled-head period I described in my previous post, but fortunately I didn't make any serious enemies.

Things have changed a lot since then, and the means of communication are different, and your personality and aspirations are probably very different. But I think there may be some some things that still apply. If you can make a personal contact with someone who has some discretion and also has an interest in helping people connect with other people, they will make you one or two introductions. If you are polite, discerning, and cautious, you can probably find your way to the people you want to meet. The people you want to meet are not the ones who are easiest to find on websites and social media. One or two of them might be commenters on JMG's blogs. You will need at least two out of these four: patience, persistence, good luck, help from a guardian spirit or deity.

I hope this helps; it's the best I can do without knowing you.

replies.title: tired of supporting the dead
replies.content:
As I read this question and answer it suddenly occurred to me that Henry VIII's dissolution of the monasteries could be seen in a similar light. One of the major tasks of monks was to perform masses for the souls of their wealthy patrons. While this did not involve putting plates of food in tombs or dressing mummies in new garments on a regular basis as in Egypt, Peru and other nations that maintained cults for dead aristocracies, it did mean setting aside money gifts, tracts of land, buildings, etc. for the purpose. Obviously, especially in the early Middle Ages, monasteries served other purposes, including charity to the poor. And Henty and his friends definitely profited from 'privatizing' all that church property. But inequality in death has to rankle when it accompanies inequality in life. Lord Oppressor gets a nice tomb and priests praying for his soul every day. Poor old Father Peasant gets a corner of the churchyard and a share of the All Souls Day prayers. Do you know of any commentary from the time that would support this view?

Rita

Hi JMG,

1. Several times in the past, you’ve mentioned that the key to all magic is learning to will a single thing strongly, with complete mental focus, for an extended period of time, and that the majority of people, instead of being conscious and aware of the consequences of their actions, instead go through life half asleep, as if in a daze.

In the course of an individual’s magical development, once they wake up and are no longer acting out of blind habit or impulse, are there any general guidelines for how to best choose goals towards which to apply the will, and to determine their worth? I imagine that journaling, meditation, and divination are involved, but does the choice of goals depend solely on a person’s subjective values, or is there some objective hierarchy of goals, with the Great Work sitting at the top and everything else flowing neatly downwards from there? I’m assuming you would personally disapprove of somebody using their newfound magical attainment to watch TV all day, but is such a choice wrong in an absolute sense?

2. Is there any difference between the application of the magical will and the concept of the ‘flow-state’ as described in the field of positive psychology? For those who are unfamiliar with it, the flow state is most likely to occur when the level of challenge offered by an activity is perfectly matched by the skill level of the participant. When someone is in the flow-state, they describe it as a state of being completely immersed in the activity, ‘in the zone’, to the point where they lose track of time, of the environment, and of themselves. This seems very close to the idea that the will, when properly applied, is effortless. Is the flow state an example of the skillful use of the will, or an unconscious abdication of it?

I get the impression that ‘using the will’ is akin to being in a self-aware, meditative state 24/7, where the attention is turned inwards on itself, and the magician is constantly monitoring their own state of mind and internal processes. However, I assume even adepts occasionally have moments when they slip into a temporary state of unconsciousness, when they’re absorbed in what they’re doing, and are no longer ‘aware of being aware’. Therefore I suspect that I’m operating under a misconception.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) In occult theory each soul has a True Will, a basic orientation toward acting in the cosmos, and one of the points of occult training is to get out of the way of that and let it express itself in a balanced and appropriate fashion in the context of one's present incarnation. Meditation, journaling, and divination are among the tools that can be used to do that. Your True Will is not mine, and so there's no one standard for everyone.

2) I have no idea. I'm not familiar with the state in question.

3) No, not at all. Nobody can maintain a self-aware meditative state 24/7 without going crazy. When you're good at using your will, you set it in motion and then get out of the way; you live your life, sleep eight hours a night, get lost in a good book from time to time, let yourself go in the arms of your lover or spouse, and so on -- but the whole time the will is in motion, and whenever you reach a situation where doing A will bring you closer to the fulfillment of your will and doing B will take you further away from it, you do A -- very often, instinctively, and sometimes without even noticing it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In his work, Carl Jung often talks about the mental life of the ‘primitive’ as compared with that of ‘civilized’ man. The terms have become dated, but I think his point still stands, that those who are less conscious are less conflicted; they have no problems because they live in the moment, immersed in the unconscious, and any difficulties they encounter produce no internal division, which leads to self-doubt and neurosis – this is similar to the mental state of most animals, young children and very elderly people. The modern man, however, is burdened with consciousness, and with that comes the burden of self-awareness, which is described as a curse in the allegorical story of the Garden of Eden.

In that view, is the goal of magic to return to this unconscious state, to remove internal division and follow the course of development that Nature has laid down for us? Are we trying to regain the primordial oneness of the Edenic state, or does magic offer the possibility of freedom, of shaping ourselves, if necessary, against the natural flow of things?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I always found those comments of Jung's -- and their many equivalents in the writings of other European intellectuals of the same era -- impressively silly. It simply isn't true that "primitive people" live in the moment, immersed in the unconscious -- the Australian aboriginal elder assessing the subtle signs that will help him guide his band across a hundred miles of gibber desert to whichever place will have the best food supply that year is using his intellect as well as his intuition; equally, the Kwakiutl matron who can recount with pride her lineage back twenty or thirty generations and the ritual privileges she has inherited from each of her ancestors is hardly lacking in self-awareness!

From the point of view of occult philosophy, certainly, the view we're discussing is utterly wrongheaded. The course of development that Nature has laid down for us leads directly to self-awareness, and the process of losing that self-awareness in late adulthood has a widely used name: senility. The natural flow of things leads us toward greater individualization, greater freedom, and greater creativity -- and it leads people of all cultures there, not just those who belong to soi-disant "civilized" cultures.

replies.title: they think different from us
replies.content:
Jung is not the first or last to attribute different levels of consciousness and mental abilities to non-European peoples. Who recalls "The Origins of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral Mind" by Julian Jaynes? I never understood the acclaim given it in some parts of the Neopagan community. Yes he argued that peoples of 3000 years ago heard the voices of gods--but he claimed that those voices were messages from one half of the brain to the other. How is that supportive of religion, the idea that gods are real and external to ourselves? Then he develops a theory that the 'breakdown' of this bicameral mind was caused by natural disasters and wars in the Mediterranean cultures. How then do you explain that peoples in other areas, such as the Americas and Australia seemed to develop the same sort of modern mind? The whole thing was logically incoherent. One Big Name Pagan I discussed it with once was very impressed by the information that the San Blas Indians had whale imagery in their art--I don't recall what relevance this had to Jayne's argument, but somehow it mattered. I pointed out that, since the San Blas indians lived on islands off the coast of Panama it would be strange if they did not have whales in their art. As for the notion that monumental projects such as the pyramids could have been constructed by a society in which everyone is getting their own 'voice of god' to follow---you couldn't build a chicken house that way. But Western Civilization, going back to the Romans at least, has a tendency to either romanticize or denigrate other cultures, particularly those that be labeled as primitive. Sometimes we manage to do both at the same time: according to Tacitus those German barbarians are a bunch of lazy drunks, but they are brave and their women are chaste.

In addition to a study of history, I would recommend a basic knowledge of cultural anthropology for a serious student of the occult. And not the antique volumes like Bachofen or Lewis H Morgan, just grab a general textbook at your nearest college bookstore.

Rita

replies.title: Re: they think different from us
replies.content: Yes, I remember Jaynes. I thought he brought together a great deal of evidence that people in many cultures talked to gods and spirits, and then completely misunderstood it because of his rationalist-materialist prejudices.

It claims that the driving human motivation is the fear and awareness of death, which sets man apart from other animals, and as a result of which we attempt to become “persons of value in a world of meaning.” In order to soothe our fear of death and forget that we are dying animals, we attempt to lead a heroic life in a symbolic world of immortal value, often enacted using ritual, thus explaining the origin of culture, mythology and religion as immortality projects. comments.title: Gurdjieff
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I appreciate your candour in recent MM’s with regard to Aleister Crowley. With that in mind, what is your take on another so-called ‘rascal master’, Gurdjieff, and the Fourth Way Movement that derives from him?

Gurdjieff seems to be gaining in popularity over the last many years through variously the Enneagram, Jacob Needleman, Parabola magazine, Jodorowsky, and even the contemplative Christian renaissance. Within the Christian Centering Prayer movement, Episcopalian Hermit Rev. Cynthia Bourgeault champions Gurdjieff as something of a world messiah and prophesies somewhere that Gurdjieff’s book ‘Beelzebub’s Tales’ will be read as scripture in churches of the future. (Gurdjieff seems to loom larger than Jesus in her own work).

On the other hand, a proponent of the Sitra Achra/Reverse Tree describes the Qlippothic intelligence of the 11th Path (to be invoked for the character of a ‘stone-cold vampiric warrior’) as a particular fan of Gurdjieff and Ouspensky’s work.

Elsewhere, Tobias Churton recently wrote of Gurdjieff as promulgating a kind of masonry for the secular intelligentsia. He ends up being a kind of 'Ayn Rand' for the spiritually inclined. Although Gurdjieff’s primary demographic seems to be aristocratically aspirational but his work seems to activate latent narcissistic, even sociopathic tendencies.

And now, a new book by Maronite priest Joseph Azize looks to be attempting to link Gurdjieff’s ideas with the hard-core Eastern Orthodox spirituality of Mt. Athos.

A lot of the Fourth Way ideas are surprising, striking and maybe even highly useful for confronting the self, but so many of the personalities representing the system come across as aloof, cold and contemptuously elitist.

That Gurdjieff is embraced with such enthusiasm by Christians at one end and magicians of darker-hued practices at another is well, curious, to say the least.
And about as likely as if key figures in the church decided that actually Crowley’s a good bet and even has the inside track on spiritual growth.

Perhaps Gurdjieff’s material is neutral and one makes of it what one wishes/wills. Perhaps it is indeed the real deal and I am missing its innate value.

What sense do you yourself make of Gurdjieff as a spiritual leader and of the Fourth Way as a system?

That said, I'm not particularly well informed on the subject; it simply didn't interest me, and I have a lot of other books by a lot of other occult philosophers to study.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is pathworking, and what is the purpose of doing it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Pathworking, also known as active imagination, might best be described as the art of focused, meditative daydreaming. In the standard sort of Pathworking, you go on an imagined journey with a specific starting point -- say, a Tarot card, which you imagine becoming a portal that opens onto the scene shown in the card. You step through the portal, wander through the landscape, talk with the beings you encounter there, and then return.

Like most magical disciplines, Pathworking has several purposes. It's a great way to strengthen your imagination and mental focus, of course. Crucially, though, your imagination is one of the ways that you interact with the astral plane, and with practice, the beings you encounter are, shall we say, less imaginary than they look; they know things you don't and can pass those things on to you. You also learn to attune yourself to the subtle energies of whatever is the focus of your Pathworking, and if you also meditate on the things you encounter in Pathworkings -- and you should always do this -- the result is a great increase in your capacity to tap into those same energies in magical workings of other kinds.

Oh, and if you have any interest in writing fiction, it's extremely useful. I don't have to invent characters and try to figure out what they do and say; I meet them in my imagination and let them do what they do!

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content:
At sunrise, burn some frankincense and pray to the Egyptian gods who are associated with the amulets, asking them to bless and empower the amulets. Then hold each amulet in the incense smoke, turning it around several times so it's touched by the smoke over its entire surface. That should do a good basic job.

There are old connections between the Celtic and Egyptian traditions. Did you know that beads from Egypt were found buried at Stonehenge? Most pagan deities work well together, but those work together more smoothly than most.

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content:
1) That's what the traditional lore claims.

Sometime after that, Druids decided to try a variant on the nemyss, the standard Golden Dawn headgear (which the GD borrowed from the Egyptians), and they found it made a really good head covering for outdoor ritual -- you can pull the ends forward and keep your neck warm, or push them back and direct any cool breeze to the back of your neck! [IMAGE:0]

replies.title: Re: Geomancy question

To Sul, who is also named Sulis--

AWEN


To Hesus, a triadic litany--

Hesus, chief of tree spirits,
R. Be with me (or us) this day, Oh God.

Thou who art chief of the tree spirits, R.
Thou who sit at the first fork of the sacred oak, R.
Thou who art master of the green world and its wisdom, R.  

Hesus, chief of tree spirits, R.

Hesus, Chief of Tree Spirits, R.

Hesus, guide of Druids, R.
Hesus, patron of Healers, R.
Hesus, teacher of Wisdom, R.

Hesus, Chief of Tree Spirits, R.

AWEN

comments.title: Learning to read at Waldorf school
comments.content:
Good morning JMG, No question at the moment, rather I wanted to comment on a question from last MM about Rudolf Steiner’s ideas about learning to read. I wasn’t able to read MM until we’ll after the doors slid shut :)

I am no authority! but both my daughters attended Waldorf school from kinder through 6th grade (not in the US where one must be very wealthy indeed to afford Waldorf school). There is a year between kinder and first grade they call pre-first (age 6) where the students learn all the letters of the alphabet and the sounds they make (in English and Spanish in this case) and how to write them. So in first grade (age 7) when they learn to read, all these puzzle pieces of reading seem to come together rather effortlessly. My daughters learned to read in Spanish before English as it is far easier to decipher from sounds. The goal here seemed to be not to just teach kids how to read but to be good readers and to enjoy reading. Waiting until 7 seemed to work out well. So perhaps they do need to make physiological connections in their brains before they are ready.

As for myself, I also taught myself to read well before kinder...but I never forced my kids just because I did it. And JMG’s comment about how learning to read super early may have some downsides certainly gave me some things to ponder!

Years of observing parents foisting things on very young children and babies like reading and math seem to always have more to do with the egos of the parents than the welfare of the children. The end result is usually very insecure, nervous children who think they are losers who can never live up to expectations. Let kids and babies be kids and babies!

Hope everyone is enjoying a productive day! SundaraYogaShala

That's the deck that inspired the one Justin uses. comments.title: Second lesson in muscle testing
comments.content:
On the other blog Scotlyn asked, “So I am very interested, but completely untutored, in this idea of using muscle testing to communicate with a body, whether it is a patient’s body (which I will seek further learning sources to improve upon) or my own body. In relation to the second, though, I wonder would it be a big ask for you to briefly illustrate with a step by step example of you DOING this? Eg. Question, technique, reaction, answer. The HOW of it. Also, if the answer sometimes might be “I don’t want to tell you” how do you distinguish that from a “yes” or a “no”?

This is a continuation of my comment from last week describing how to use muscle testing to test physical imbalances currently in one’s body rather than trying to test questions being held in one’s mind (that will be next week.) When practicing muscle testing on yourself (without a partner) all the principles presented last week for testing with another person are still applicable. You will still be following your hunches, and testing out your hypotheses. You can also continue to hold a point that tests weak while looking for another point that strengthens that imbalance, though that will require a fair amount of dexterity. You can narrow down from broad, full-hand contact to just a fingertip in order to speed up your testing. You will still benefit from trying acupressure first on any point of imbalance before trying more complex rebalancing techniques. You can still test whether anything that presses on or distorts your body ends up balancing or imbalancing you. And your muscles or your patience may get exhausted before you can finish decoding a complex imbalance, requiring you to take a break and retrace your steps when you are ready to resume testing. If any of those principles are confusing, review last week’s comment for clarification.

Muscle testing physical imbalances on your own body involves keeping conscious control over the muscles of the arm that is touching points as well as the arm that is applying pressure to do the test, but somehow relinquishing just enough control over the muscle being tested to both maintain constant resistance and allow it to weaken when the rest of the body does. That leaves a lot of room for psychic gremlins to make considerable mischief. Remember, it's an art, not a science! I highly recommend practicing muscle testing with a partner before you try testing by yourself. When being tested by or testing another, the subject will only have to try to hold his or her muscle at constant strength, letting the aide do the pushing. This spreads the complexity out across two people, which is very useful when beginning the practice. Plus it can be fun to share with a like-minded explorer. With two people as witness, it is also more likely that one or both will pick up on subtle weakening or the odd effects of willful attempts by the subject to not allow their body to weaken (to control the test.) We all have our own ways of trying to control life and of lying to ourselves, so no simple example could cover all the ways that trying to control the test could manifest. We’re much more complicated than that.

As I mentioned last month, I use one of my iliopsoas muscles to do solo testing. The iliopsoas is primary in lifting the leg and stabilizing the body in walking. It is one of the strongest muscles in the body, so it does not tire quickly. Different positions will allow greater or lesser ease in reaching different testing points. If you feel unsafe trying to reach an awkward point (the middle of your back) or keep your balance while testing, do partnered testing for that point instead. Straining or injuring yourself is not the goal here, nor should solo muscle testing feel like a yoga workout. What I do while standing is flex either thigh forward to around 90° with my knee bent, foreleg dangling. Then I use the palm or knuckles of the fist of my same-side arm to press down on my lifted thigh close to its knee to test its strength. I use my opposite-side arm to touch points suspected of imbalance, while using my same-side arm again to test my thigh’s strength. Testing points on the lower leg is awkward (if not dangerous) while standing, so I test them while sitting or lying down to reach my lower leg or foot. What I do while lying on my back looks identical to the standing test but in a supine position. I flex either thigh forward to around a 90° bend with my knee bent, foreleg parallel to the floor and test as above.

What I do while sitting is flex either thigh up a little higher than the normal 90° bend of the thigh resting on the chair and press down on my thigh close to my knee with my same side-hand, knuckles or even elbow in particularly awkward positions. With the hip flexed so high the muscle will be less strong in resisting being pushed down. Depending on your balance/strength, you may find it helps to lean back against the chair back so that lifting the thigh off the seat only brings it up to 90°. Eventually you want to be able to test while seated upright or even bent forward as this position allows you to test awkward points on your leg or foot. To test points on my arm or hand, I usually sit and just touch points on my same-side arm that is doing the pressing/testing of my thigh. The physical description sounds awfully complex, but once you get a sense of what your own strengthening/weakening effect feels like, you can play around with finding which muscle works best for you to test with. You may well find an easier way to muscle test for your body.

The main difficulty that muscle testing on yourself presents is getting your personality out of the way enough to let your body strengthen and weaken on its own (not under your willful control,) while still rationally analyzing and comparing its responses to the pains and imbalances you are feeling. This is no easier for professional body workers, athletes, or coaches than for anyone with less body training. After all, the entity you need to have training and discipline in reining in here is your personality, not your body. Ever met an MD who seemed to have surprisingly limited practice reigning in his run-away ego? That skill falls outside his area of training and expertise. Imagine how hard it could be for famous athletes or surgeons to let their bodies tell them that they can no longer be relied on to perform perfectly as trained, no matter how much willful control they try to exert. There are far too many instances of promising draft picks giving in to despair and drink after an injury, rather than getting to know their new injured body and working with it as a perfectly willing, but limited partner. Many body “experts” have gained deep understanding and experience of the body being wiser in many ways than the mind, but just as many have developed nothing but arrogant disdain for all the fragile, dysfunctioning bodies they have had (and often wasted) the amazing opportunity to partner with.

All of us have had those magical experiences where our body’s natural intelligence takes over, and the mind becomes an observer going along for the ride. Some of us may have no memory of having that experience since being children, but it is happening for all of us every day. The problem is that we are rarely aware of our bodies’ intelligence, coordination, and mastery. We can walk for miles enjoying the birdsong and dappled sunlight in perfect coordination, but most of us will become awkward, self-conscious disasters the moment we turn our attention to our gait, stride, arm swing, etc. Trained athletes are no exception, and most lose their mastery the moment they start thinking about it.

How do you get out of the way of your body’s skill, while actively paying close attention to its awareness and wisdom? You may be one of the few who always did that naturally (and I am truly jealous,) or you may have practiced long and hard to discipline your awareness to be able to ponder what is outside you, without changing its behavior or frightening it away. The still patience of bird watching is a marvelous metaphor for observing your body just being itself. If you find muscle testing on yourself or even being the subject of muscle testing with someone else to be difficult, begin paying attention to your body as it does the everyday things it does — washing dishes, pushing a wheelbarrow, balancing on a moving train or bus. The mystery of the coordination of instincts that keeps us from constantly falling down is truly worthy of contemplation! And be gentle with yourself. Beating yourself up for completely bollixing up your gait as soon as you begin focusing on it is decidedly unhelpful. That would be like getting angry each time you can’t see a bird in the treetops that an advanced bird watcher has pointed to. Each time you try, you are developing the endless skills that will one day allow you to point out unseen birds to others.

— Christophe

comments.title: Belly Heat
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG

I've got some questions about my subtle body.

My belly (my whole abdomen, really, but particularly around my navel) is almost always significantly hotter to the touch than the rest of my body, and my feet tend to be cool. Now I always thought this was normal and just because of how far my hands and feet are from my heart, and there relatively high surface area, but apparently other people have a more even distribution of heat. I'm male, for what it's worth.

I run hot in general, especially when I sleep. I'd rather be in a cold environment than a hot one, almost any day. And feet are not uncomfortable, just markedly cooler than my belly. I had never even noticed the difference until my girlfriend was touching me and pointed it out.

Perhaps relatedly, I eat a lot, and basically non-stop. Because of this, I'm not often hungry. I'm not fat - I maintain about 190lbs at just over 6 feet. But for years people have remarked on my eating habits, both in regards to quantity and consistency of eating. I also am thirstier than most people, and drink a lot of water.

I started thinking about this after a recent shamanic healing session in which the practitioner mentioned how much fire I have, and located the fire in my Cauldron of the Earth and related it to the dragons from the Inner Grail Working. (I had told her about my work in the Dolmen Arch, specifically the SoP and Inner Grail Working). Now that I think of it, the Cauldron of the Earth is also named the Cauldron of Warming. This healer is the one who told me that the dragons "were eager to help but needed a job," which prompted my recent Magic Monday question. (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/81997.html?thread=7754829#cmt7754829) She was really insistent that I had a lot of heat, and needed to temper it or put it to use.

I would not have thought of myself as fiery, but she's not the first person to contradict that self-perception.
